Hey, it's Andre and Sean. Today we are talking about story powered promotions. This is, the story powered promotion really is the workhorse of the art of email. It is one of two parts of the revenue engine. And as you know, as we've discussed before, the art of email is built on sort of a trust engine and a revenue engine. And they're not two distinct things to be clear. There's a lot of connectivity. It's not an either or, it's not a hey, we're working on trust in relationships or we're trying to sell. I mean, the both things permeate each other, particularly the relationship focus permeates the selling focus. I'm going to, Andre is going to give us an overview in a moment. But before we do that, I want to, to take some of the things, some of the questions that I had about the story powered promotion initially and put them together for you as sort of a frame to think about. The first thing to understand about a story powered promotion is that don't get hung up on the, on story. This is a, this is a place where we see people go wrong because they, they make story to be capital S really involved in all I need a character and the character needs to have a goal. It's not, don't think about story that way. A story could be something that could be a book you read or an excerpt from a book or a conversation you had in an elevator somewhere that just kind of sets things up or that it can be the theme that, that goes from beginning to end, not just the frame. And there's so many ways to think about story that story is the entry point. It's not, it doesn't have to be that complicated. It could be some experience that you had in the past. That experience may not necessarily even have to relate to something you did professionally or it could. But story is just, it's really module to principles of serialized narratives. That's really what we're talking about is that you're having a multi email conversation with an audience and that audit, that multi email conversation is bound together into this larger structure. And that structure is a story. And that could be the story about, Hey, how I found out about XYZ and why I want to share that with you. The story could be, I had this thing happen to me. And that thing is kind of like this other thing. And let me tell you about that. And then everything that follows. And there are so many different ways to think about this. It just don't get hung up on the use of the word story. And one last thing before I turn it over to Andre, this, we want to be clear about this when we say a story power promotion, we are, we are expecting in general that the story is true. Now, I want to be clear about what we mean when we say that. More, more than anything else, we're not saying go invent stories that sell things to people. That's not at all what we're talking about. It's, but when we say that they need to be true, that doesn't mean that everything that you talk about has to be something that you did. And it's this long, complex thing. It's just that the, what you're talking about, there's truth to it. That's really what we want. Maybe it's a book that you read. It's a conversation that you had. It's a movie that you were inspired by that reminded you of something else. There are so many places to look for inspiration for stories, but we're never suggesting that you use the power of story to manipulate an audience. That's not at all what we need. It's the story becomes the spine that carries a long conversation over, you know, five, seven, 10 emails. It's the thing that binds those together, that frames them, that creates the narrative arc that creates the theme. That's what we're talking about. So with all of that is a disjointed background, Andre, I'll hand it over to you to clean that up and make more sense. No pressure. Alrighty. So this is the large image and we've just zoomed into the top so we can go through this sequentially. So the revenue engine, as you probably know, is made up of two components. One's a time limited. So product launch series is when you do a promotion, there's a beginning and there's an end on a certain date. You've seen us do many of these before. And then the other side of that is the story part promotions, which is a similar thing, but it's evergreen, which means, you know, you plug them in and they work. And the bridges that get people into here are almost always evergreen as well, in the sense that they, within the context of an email series. So these things can get kicked off anytime and you never have to be aware of it other than you've coded it up and you've put it inside there. So just like an obvious and an SOS, it's a sequence of emails that has a beginning and an end. However, unlike every other variant, it's controlled by some automation that then figures out if somebody has already raised their hand to be promoted this thing. So let me back up slightly. Whenever we send out a PLS, so a product launch series, those people that don't purchase, even though they've raised their hand, well, they may raise their hand in the future when we promote that same product again. So if it's an open, if it's an open close thing that that happens multiple times a year, the next time around, somebody will raise their hand. Well, some of those people could have raised their hand the first time around. And now they're getting this for a second time. But at no point is that promotion going to be identical to the first one. It's almost always different. The themes might be changed. The, you know, some of the context is changed. How we frame that thing. We've learned something from the previous one we've done. So it's always changing and evolving. Well, story power promotions do exactly the same thing, but it's obviously automated. So we have automation that controls which one somebody gets. So here in the bottom left here, this is a basic idea of what a single sequence would look like. There would be a tag that gets somebody in. There'll be an automation that controls it. And in this case here, it's just a single sequence. So it'll, it'll just flow in simply like that. But the distinction is that there is an automation that controls this, even when there's just a single one. And let me jump in here for a second, too, because I had lots of questions about this. I really can play the role of the audience in this one. And I think having had these conversations, I can, I can put this into some sort of context. So let's say for the example for us, that we have, we have open enrollment on four of our core courses. So yes, sometimes we do, we might do a PLS if there's something that's time bound, like the live workshop for something. But in general, we're not doing product launch series because there's not no hard open and close. So the story power promotion responds to the context that somebody has in our world, independent of something that we're doing other than showing them that we have this value that's out there. So we could have, for example, a newsletter and that newsletter could say, you know, something about the topic of the newsletter could be paid traffic, for example, and we could, it might be, for example, recently there's been a development with iOS 15 and it's changed email. And that's also their privacy settings that affect paid traffic. And we could, we could write a newsletter about that. And at the bottom have a hard bridge that says, you know, if you're interested in learning more about paid traffic, we, we have a course called the traffic engine and we unpack over seven days, the fundamentals of paid traffic and how we explain them in the traffic engine. And that somebody could click on that and then they could see a story powered promotion. You know, they would, there's a bridge, they would get something, they would get an email, if it's a hard bridge, they might get an email that says, hey, we've seen you raise your hand, you're interested in the traffic engine, we have this seven part series. And if you'd like to read that, click here and we could, you can understand sort of the architecture. But then what happens is they get this series and some significant percentage of people who go through that series are not going to buy. Like that's just, that's how it always works. And we don't know why, if context matter, you know, everything, and this is all the parts, you know, interrelated, but we don't know why that person didn't buy at that moment. But what may happen is that same person three months later, there may be some significant change in his or her life, maybe her business, all of a sudden paid traffic is in need again. And because we've been maintaining this relationship, that person may raise his or her hand again and say in a different prompt, I need to figure out this paid traffic thing. And they, when they do that, what we don't want to do is just send them the thing that we already sent them. The, and this automation that Andre is referring to is the thing that manages that. It manages the eventuality that no matter, you know, no matter how good we are, not everybody's going to buy everything that we offer every single time we offer it to them, even when they raise their hand and say they're interested. So the automation controls that. And essentially it's someone in the background who's thinking like, oh, OK, I see that you saw the first one and you didn't buy. So now we're going to move on to the next one in the stack. I love that term. There's a stack of story-powered promotions. So now we're going to move on to the next one and we're going to continue that journey. And you as the author have the benefit also of knowing that not only has that person raised their hand, but when they're reading the second one, they've already seen the first one. So you have this additional context. So that, a lot of that took me a while to wrap my head around. So thanks for giving me the latitude here, Andre, to jump in and sort of explain how that made sense to me after you explained it. So take it with that, with that sort of tangential spin, jump into where you were headed next. OK, so before we get to the proper stacks, we just want to explain this thing called well that we refer to as a VIP. So in the case that there's only a single, a single sequence in a stack, depending on your resources and your time constraints, it's not going to be easy to create multiple promotions for any one product. And if you've got 10 products, well, you know, that's going to be a lot of emails to write. So this little extra piece here is a piece that it's just. So in this case here, it's a single thing. And if somebody like raises their hand again a second time around, as Sean mentioned, the automation is going to prevent them from receiving the same thing. But because there's only one inside here, well, that's a problem because they're going to click a link and nothing's going to happen. You can certainly set it up when they click a link. It sends in the same one again. But that's not great. So this little red dot over here, it recognizes that someone's been through this. And then it sends them a single email and we give some examples of what that looks like in the lesson. So we won't go over that now. But that single email, it's just recognizing that there have been three. It says, hey, I noticed that you raised your hand again. We sent you this thing previously. If you really want to receive it again, just click here and we'll send it over to you. So that in this in this case, that'll work for even though you've got a single one. So then you can't keep looking people around if you really wanted to. Or if you just didn't have the time to write the second one and the third one at that point in time. So this thing is kind of like a catch all, but it just gives people some context. So so they don't think, well, this is weird. I'm sure I've seen this already before. The exact same thing. But by sending them that email, you're just letting them know that you're going to resend them the same thing. But in most cases, you you you're not going to have a single one. Or if you do, it's not going to stay single for for very long. So I'm going to scroll down here. Now we get to the workhorse part of this before you jump into the workhorse. So I want to I want to do a use scenario that you you talked about in the actual modules. And I think it's really great. So if I'm and this is particularly important, if you have lots of courses and you want to create stacks for every course, there's a certain time commitment for that. Or if you're, you know, maybe you're it's maybe you're. We see this happen a lot. There's a single business owner who has either a small team or some some contractors who work for him or her and that you're kind of doing a lot of the work yourself. And you create a story of power promotion. And you and you and you there's other stuff to do, frankly, right? And so instead of going immediately to the to creating the second story, power promotion to use that VIP email as a as a door. And you you mentioned this in the specifically in the in the module content where that email could go to somebody and say, hey, I noticed that you you've already been through this series and you're interested, you're still interested. So, you know, here's a link to my calendar, you know, to Calendly order. Here's a link to my calendar. Let's grab 15 minutes and let me just see if I can answer any questions that you might have. Like that's for a small business. That's a that's a really great way to triple the value of a story of power promotion without a lot of work. And then what you might do is when you if you can think about the evolution over time is maybe you have six or seven of those conversations and you realize, OK, I'm going to go back and I'm going to update my original SPP. So I answer those questions. And then I'm also going to so I don't have that'll get as many of these questions. I'm going to write a second story of power promotion. But after the second story of power promotion in the stack, if somebody has come back now a third time, then I'm going to take that VIP email and put it on the end of three. And that's I think what you're getting to here. It's it's just such a beautiful idea that it's this one extra thing that can scale with your story powered promotion stack that wherever you are in your business, it grows with you. And it could be after one story powered promotion. It could be after six story powered promotions, but it can always be there as this extra layer of value that essentially says I've done everything I can so far to explain this thing that I have that's valuable and you're still not ready for it. So let's just jump on a call. Let me see if how I can help you. That's it's such a valuable addition. So I'll hand that back to you now. Yeah, so that's a great point. And we give a few examples in inside of the module. The other thing that it does is as you said, it's a feedback loop that allows you to make every subsequent SPP better where there's some intelligence where you're learning something so you're not just winging it and making or making up the second one. You know, you're just trying a different angle and you're just trying things out. So one thing that we haven't mentioned in this in this module. So I'll I'll mention it now is that doing time limited promotion, so PLS style promotions, which are always love in the moment. So Sean and I, we did that. We've we did one to get help, talk to people into this. Well, we do that love and we are in the moment and we're receiving emails and people are responding and there's this love feedback loop that happens. Once that's done and dusted and that whole promotion is finished, we've got results from it. We can see what worked very well. We could see maybe we could do us do a few things better or maybe a certain angle didn't quite resonate about another one did. All of that gets put into your first story powered promotion. All of that intelligence is gets hard coded into you as an evergreen version. So most people's first story powered promotion will be stuff that they've got or learned from a from a hard open and close. And again, it doesn't have to be hard open and close in the sense that it's it's it's a it's a promotion that's been that's that's done using broadcast. So you've sent it out and you get data back from it. So they'll be the first one and you're learning stuff. But then once it's inside of here now, well, there's no mechanism to really learn other than people responding to this thing, this this automated event here. So that's what this little red dot is good for. So in this case, yeah, these stacks are for products. So products or courses, this could be out of email, the durable business traffic engine, for example. And inside of here is full promotions. And what this automation does is that somebody comes in here, it checks to see if they've received any of them. If they haven't received any of them, they get the first one. Next time around, if they happen to come back inside here again, it'll check on that we received that one. So it's going to send them the second one. But the beautiful thing with this from a creator's point of view, so it's from us creating these things is we have context in the sense that we when you when we write the second promotion, we know that somebody that that person receiving the second one has already received the first one. So that gives us context like whatever the frame was for this, whatever the the angle. Well, it's going to be different over here and you can you can you can use this. You can talk about stuff inside of here within the context of this. And vice versa, if somebody is receiving this one, well, you know, they've they haven't purchased in the last two attempts. So this promotion here is going to look and feel very different. And then obviously, finally, like we've already mentioned, if somebody doesn't respond the third time around, they get this this VIP one. And we give an example of what what that could look like in the context of a of a bigger stack. And, you know, it's it's it's it's very elegant. The way it's done, it's very personal. The idea is to is to is to ideally get somebody on call and then learn why they didn't, you know, what what did we say? That is going to help us to edit one of these, all of these and address that that person's concern. So then for every single other person that goes through there, this these things are going to get better and better over time and stronger and stronger and more and more powerful. Is anything else you want to you want to add, Sean? A couple of things. One. You know, they mentioned at the very beginning about, you know, what what is the story about? Well, oftentimes if a story powered promotion follows, eventually, a product launch series, the first story that you're telling can be the story of the product launch series. It's sometimes you have a built in story and it doesn't it doesn't have to be the verbatim. But you can think about it. Your your first story powered promotion most likely is informed by the product launch series that launched that promotion originally. So you you automatically have a built in story to tell the story is, you know, I created this thing that whenever you created it and you can you can it's self or self referential that you have your first story powered promotions almost always have a built in story that you can tell because it's the thing that already happened. Now, you may have courses already or you may have products where how you're using the art of you know, you may have something already that is far away from the original PLS. So you don't have that to work from. But whatever the telling the story about it can come from really any angle. And you can think about this in many different ways. You can think about it as a single theme. You can think about as being character driven. Perhaps you have a a customer who purchased at a certain point in the past and he or she just went through it and got this incredible result. And the way that they did that is is the underlying story. You can refer back to that person a lot or maybe it's several people. And the theme of the story powered promotion is how other people how they got the most results from it. I mean, the sky is the limit. I keep coming back to this because I know that we can very easily get stuck, especially when we look at a screen like this and think, wow, I've got four courses and I've got three things for each course. Like it can seem like a lot, but it's. You're building it one step at a time. And in each time you add that layer, you're adding more value to the whole system, which makes it makes it very powerful. A good friend of mine this weekend said something that I just love. This is not in reference to the story powered promotion, but the story powered promotion is really where we get to that point where our business is making money where we when we sleep, right? This is happening. The story powered promotion is happening in our business when we're not having to write the email that day, push, send. Like it's happening automatically based on user behavior. But the comment my friend made this weekend was that, you know, if you want to have a business that makes money while you sleep, you need to be willing to do some work when you're awake, right? So, yes, this is going to create a business for you that makes money for you when you sleep. It's it's doing the work of your business 24 seven, 365. But you do have to do some work when you're awake to get to this stage. So, yes, this looks pretty daunting. But when you get in and you write your let's just say you do have four things that you sell right your first story powered promotion for each one and add the VIP automation to each of those and then come back to one and rates your second one in this. Yeah, I'll jump in with an example. It's not exactly what we did, but it can show how we were able to do it. But it can show how we could take this example and make it real. So in the fall of 2020, we did an enrollment. And that the enrollment was for three of our courses. And we use the theme of that enrollment was building a durable business and durable business has all of the components working together. And in those components, our awareness, engagement and conversion. So the structure of that was we did five emails that didn't talk about our products at all. And we were very clear over those five emails that everybody was going to get this series. And then if anyone who raised their hand, we had a hard bridge link in those emails. Anyone who raised their hand who was interested in more would get the next five the following week, where we were going to talk about our products and map our products to these ideas. And we could do something similar here or you're using any example for any business is we could have. We could do this with or without a hard bridge. Here we have a hard bridge. So we could if you think about it in terms of as it's written, we could have a value newsletter and we can say, you know what? We've had paid traffic on our mind for a while now. There are lots of things have happened in the last six months. And we want to put all of that together into a series that's that we're calling it the X, Y, Z paid traffic series. It's it's five emails over five days. And it's really an update on like how you need to think about paid traffic now that iOS 15 has been released or something like that. And then someone who raises his or her hand is raising their hand because of what we just described. They're not raising their hand in that context to say, sell me something. They're raising their hand saying, I'm interested in this topic. So that showcase series, we could we could do exactly what we said. You know, we're talking about iOS 15 and how it affects paid traffic. And we could include in a PS somewhere. If you're looking for the definitive course on paid traffic, we created the traffic engine. You can learn more here, whatever we could put that in there. So it might generate some money initially. But what that showcase series would do would be we know that we're talking about paid traffic to an audience interested in paid traffic. We have their attention and it could lead to using the traffic engine as an example throughout. And when they got to the end, the majority of people who have gone through that series probably aren't interested in some percentage of them have purchased the traffic engine as a result of that series, some small percentage. But at the end of it, there can be another hard bridge that says, hey, that's the end of our five part series on paid traffic. We have it. If you're if you're interested in more and how we think about paid traffic in light of everything that we just shared with you in this series, then you can learn more about the traffic engine. And we have it. You know, we have a five part series where we go deep into how the traffic engine maps to the current reality of paid traffic. And that then kicks off again. And if you think about it, it's increasing levels of specificity moving toward a product with explicit permission from the audience, who's initially said, tell me more about the topic. And now there is an answer, not only do I want to know more about the topic, but now that you've explained the topic to me, I want to know more about how you think about it with your course about that topic. And you can you can imagine how that and when you take it to all the potential automation, you can see that over time someone, so perhaps somebody goes through the showcase series and they are interested in the course and they see the first story power promotion. But for whatever reason, the context is wrong for them and they don't buy. And then later, because we've set the stage, we've given them lots of value about paid traffic in general. And later, their context changes and they're like, oh, I need to think about paid traffic again. I've got to go back and they indicate interest again. Well, then we send them the second in the showcase series. And you can just see how all of these pieces work together to give the end user the best possible experience. But without us having to manage each user's experience so that we dilute the quality for the whole, we're giving very specific value to each individual based on how their behavior is telling us through an automated system, how to give them the most value that's most most useful to them in their current context. That's really that that's so incredibly important. I mean, I can't overemphasize how important that is. And anything else to wrap up? This has been a long one. This is a long module, but anything else you want to wrap up here? And there's the full image right there. We can see the complexity in its glory. Yeah, I think the only extra thing I'll add is as you go through the art of email, it should become more and more obvious. Certainly in this lesson as well, in this module, that you can create something that's very nuanced and unique. So your version, your expression of an art of email system will look like nobody else's on the planet that has been through this course. You know, you get to assemble each little thing. You get to assemble it in a way that matters to you and how you want to show up in the world and deliver value. Our versions are going to be different to yours. None will ever be the same. It's impossible. It's just there's so many different options. And once you understand the building blocks of and understand how these things work, you can assemble them in really unique ways, ways that we haven't even demonstrated over here because, you know, there's no way we can show every single, you know, nuance. And even the these little VIP emails here, just the idea of it can, there's many different expressions of what that could be and the different things you could do with that. So I think that's that's my final word on this. Yeah, I'll just take that one. I'll just run with that too. And I'll stop talking. But it's that's the thing that has been most impressive to me throughout this entire journey. Creating the art of email, co-creating the art of email and really thinking out loud about some of the things that we're talking about is that it's it's what it the art of email is like 50 layers deep. I mean, there's the you go through it once you get the idea that, okay, relationships matter more than anything else. And if you focus on building relationships, the sales or emergent properties of those well developed relationships, like, okay, that's that's a very important concept. And everything that follows reflects that at a basic level. So somebody could go through this, create a relationship building series and building I have a story power promotion occasionally do PLS's and get tremendous value from the art of email. But then there's this extra layer where it's just infinitely deep that you take the ideas and you not you can use the ideas the way we're sharing them. But then you can also take the ideas and take the spirit of the ideas and go places that we can't even imagine. And that that to me is what's shocking. We'll get into some of those into advanced ideas. You've some that we've thought about, but there's just in just what we've talked about in story power promotions. There are so many ways that you could use this. You could you could spend years building a business just on the idea of a value newsletter, hard bridge, SOS and story powered promotion stacked in different ways with different responses, depending on how somebody is interacting with them. Like you could just spend years and years and years building out a system that produces results beyond anything that you could produce. If you were just sending you know, sending your daily email trying to figure it out that the system is so responsive to the needs of the audience. And because it evolves over time, it creates an effect that it's not even predictable. It's an exponential improvement that is not something that we can even predict how it will go. Even at just the face of how we're describing it without all of the layers of complexity that you can add specific to your business. So it's it's so easy, especially once we get into the story of power promotion where we're really beginning to bring things together. This is where I think we can really begin to see that. Oh my God moment where this this is wildly wildly different. And this is the jumping off point, I think, for everyone participating in the art of the mother go, Oh, now I understand how complex this can be to create results for my business, not complex to create complex and it's in its in its recognition of the reality that everybody in our audience is different. And everybody in our audience is going to have a slightly different context, a slightly different need, and we can create a system that responds to their needs. individually in ways that will produce results that we never could imagine by trying to deliberately create those results on our own. Alright, anything else to add to that? Shall we wrap up for today? No, I think that's it. In fact, as you were saying that I've thought of a metaphor that at least in my head makes sense, but it might be a bad metaphor to think about. But it's almost like like a game of chess, you know, or the game of chess, you know, it's really easy to to to understand the basic moves and moving your your your pieces around. But as we know, you know, it's it's infinitely complex in in how you can express chess or how you can learn chess. So this is not exactly like you this person is that person, but in terms of you can you can shape this and express this in in ways way beyond what we present here. That's it, Rudy. Yeah, I think that's a beautiful metaphor. I mean, I don't I don't play chess, but I understand the idea which, you know, chess seems simple. You know, when you first look at it, like each, each part of the each part of the board moves a certain way, each piece on the board moves a certain way. But because they move certain ways, there's the way it moves. And then there's the way it moves relative to other pieces. And you get this infinitely and then you have number of moves that happen and there's a back and forth. So you get this emergent complexity. From a simple set of pieces. I think that's a perfect metaphor for the art of email because we were giving the everyone in the audience the like these are the chess pieces. Here's the chessboard. Here's how the pieces move in relation to each other. And there's an infinitely expandable universe of ways you can make those pieces interact for your audience to produce results that we can't even imagine in advance. Yeah, that's a now I want to learn how to play chess. I can appreciate the metaphor. Excellent. Alright, thanks everybody. We appreciate it. As we say every time we finish up a module, we'll get back to work.
